class_schedule.topic: Scope Definition and the Work Breakdown Structure
learning_objectives: Develop a scope statement from a project charter
Apply decomposition techniques to create a WBS
Define work packages with measurable acceptance criteria

lecture_notes.introduction: Planning begins with scope. If you do not know what you are building, no schedule or budget will save you.
key_concepts: Scope statement: deliverables, exclusions, constraints, assumptions
Decomposition: breaking deliverables into work packages
WBS levels: project, deliverable, work package, activity
WBS dictionary entries: description, owner, duration estimate, cost estimate
Scope baseline = approved scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live creation of a WBS for an office relocation project using a hierarchical chart

review.value: Subdivide deliverables until work packages are small enough to estimate duration and cost reliably (8-80 hour rule)
The approved scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary serve as the reference for all scope-related decisions

learning_objectives: Identify mandatory, discretionary, and external dependencies
Construct a network diagram using precedence diagramming method (PDM)

lecture_notes.introduction: Once scope is defined, we break work packages into schedulable activities and determine their logical relationships.
key_concepts: Activity vs. work package: activities are the schedulable units
Dependency types: finish-to-start, start-to-start, finish-to-finish, start-to-finish
Mandatory dependencies: inherent in the nature of the work
Discretionary dependencies: preferred sequences based on best practice
External dependencies: outside the project team's control
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drawing a precedence diagram for a website redesign project with 15 activities

review.value: Most common dependency: successor cannot start until predecessor finishes
Lead accelerates the successor; lag inserts a delay between activities

learning_objectives: Apply analogous, parametric, and three-point estimating methods
Calculate PERT estimates using optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values
Understand the relationship between estimation accuracy and project phase

lecture_notes.introduction: Estimation is both art and science. Accurate estimates require historical data, expert judgment, and structured techniques.
key_concepts: Analogous estimating: uses historical data from similar projects
Parametric estimating: uses statistical relationships (e.g., cost per square foot)
Three-point estimate: (O + 4M + P) / 6 for expected duration
Standard deviation: (P - O) / 6 for individual activities
Cone of uncertainty: estimates refine as the project progresses
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating PERT estimates and standard deviations for a set of construction activities

review.value: Expected = (O + 4M + P) / 6; gives weighted average favoring the most likely estimate
Early estimates may range -25% to +75%; after detailed planning, range narrows to -5% to +10%

learning_objectives: Perform forward and backward pass calculations
Identify the critical path and calculate total float
Analyze the impact of delays on the project end date

lecture_notes.introduction: The critical path is the longest path through the network and determines the minimum project duration. Activities on this path have zero float.
key_concepts: Forward pass: calculates early start (ES) and early finish (EF) for each activity
Backward pass: calculates late start (LS) and late finish (LF) from the end
Total float = LS - ES = LF - EF
Free float: amount an activity can be delayed without affecting its successor
Multiple critical paths and near-critical paths
lecture_notes.demonstration: Step-by-step CPM calculation on a 12-activity network with hand-drawn diagrams

review.value: The longest path through the network; any delay on a critical activity delays the project
The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project end date

learning_objectives: Apply crashing and fast-tracking techniques to compress the schedule
Perform resource leveling to resolve over-allocation
Evaluate trade-offs between schedule compression and cost

lecture_notes.introduction: When the calculated schedule exceeds the deadline, we must compress it. When resources are over-allocated, we must level them.
key_concepts: Crashing: adding resources to critical path activities to reduce duration
Fast-tracking: performing sequential activities in parallel (increases risk)
Crash cost per unit time: (crash cost - normal cost) / (normal duration - crash duration)
Resource leveling: adjusting the schedule to resolve resource conflicts
Resource smoothing: optimizing resource usage without extending the critical path
lecture_notes.demonstration: Crashing analysis on a network with cost data, selecting optimal activities to crash

review.value: Always crash the critical path activity with the lowest crash cost per time unit first
May extend the project duration; resource smoothing keeps the end date but has limits

class_schedule.topic: Building and Maintaining the Project Schedule
learning_objectives: Build a complete project schedule in Microsoft Project
Apply schedule control techniques to manage variances
readings: Microsoft Project Step by Step, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: The schedule is a living document. It must be baselined, monitored, and updated throughout the project.
key_concepts: Gantt chart construction: tasks, durations, dependencies, milestones
Setting the schedule baseline: freezing the approved schedule for comparison
Schedule variance (SV) = EV - PV; Schedule Performance Index (SPI) = EV / PV
Schedule updates: re-estimating remaining work, adjusting dependencies
Trend analysis and forecasting using SPI
lecture_notes.demonstration: End-to-end schedule build in Microsoft Project for a product launch project
setup_requirements: Completed WBS and activity list from earlier sessions

review.value: The approved version of the schedule against which actual performance is measured
SPI > 1.0 means ahead of schedule; SPI < 1.0 means behind schedule

questions.question: Given the following three-point estimates for Activity X: Optimistic = 4 days, Most Likely = 7 days, Pessimistic = 16 days, calculate the PERT expected duration and the standard deviation.
questions.answer: Expected duration = (4 + 4(7) + 16) / 6 = (4 + 28 + 16) / 6 = 48 / 6 = 8 days. Standard deviation = (16 - 4) / 6 = 12 / 6 = 2 days.

questions.question: Explain the difference between mandatory and discretionary dependencies and provide one example of each.
questions.answer: Mandatory dependencies are inherent in the nature of the work and cannot be changed (e.g., you must pour a foundation before building walls). Discretionary dependencies are based on best practices or team preferences and can be modified (e.g., choosing to complete design reviews before starting development, even though some development could begin in parallel).

quizzes.topic: Critical Path Method and Schedule Compression

questions.question: Given a network with paths A-B-C (durations 3, 5, 4 = 12 days) and A-D-E (durations 3, 6, 2 = 11 days), identify the critical path and calculate the total float for Activity D.
questions.answer: The critical path is A-B-C with a duration of 12 days. For Activity D: ES=3, EF=9. LS=4, LF=10. Total float = LS - ES = 4 - 3 = 1 day.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies A-B-C as critical path
Correct forward/backward pass for Activity D
Correct total float calculation of 1 day

questions.question: A project manager must reduce the project duration by 3 days. Activity F (critical, normal duration 10 days, crash duration 7 days, crash cost $6,000) and Activity G (critical, normal duration 8 days, crash duration 6 days, crash cost $8,000). Which activity should be crashed first and why?
questions.answer: Activity F should be crashed first because its crash cost per day is $6,000 / 3 days = $2,000/day, while Activity G's crash cost per day is $8,000 / 2 days = $4,000/day. Always crash the activity with the lowest cost per unit of time saved first.

rubric.key: Correct crash cost per day for both activities
Selects Activity F as the first to crash

major_assignments.title: Complete Project Schedule Development
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete project schedule for a medium-complexity project (30-50 activities) including WBS, network diagram, CPM analysis, resource assignments, and Gantt chart.
objectives: Build a comprehensive WBS with at least 30 activities
Construct a network diagram with all dependency types
Perform CPM analysis identifying critical path and float
deliverables: CPM calculation showing ES, EF, LS, LF, and float for all activities
Microsoft Project file with Gantt chart, resource assignments, and baseline
Schedule narrative explaining key decisions and assumptions (2-3 pages)

criteria: WBS follows 100% rule and has appropriate decomposition levels
Work packages have clear descriptions and acceptance criteria
WBS dictionary is complete for all work packages
Network diagram correctly shows all dependencies
Forward and backward pass calculations are correct
Float values are accurate for all activities
Gantt chart accurately reflects the network
Resources are assigned and over-allocations resolved
Baseline is set and schedule is realistic
Narrative clearly explains assumptions and decisions
Risks to the schedule are identified
Correct CPM analysis with all calculations shown
Professional Gantt chart with resource leveling

weekly_quizzes.description: Biweekly quizzes on scheduling concepts, calculations, and techniques

midterm_examination.description: CPM calculations, network diagrams, and estimating techniques

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all scheduling and planning topics

lab_exercises.description: In-class exercises using Microsoft Project and scheduling tools

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: The schedule project is individual work. Study groups for exam preparation are encouraged but all submitted work must be original.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all sources. Plagiarism will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Microsoft Project (or ProjectLibre) is required. Campus computer labs have Microsoft Project installed. Students should bring laptops to lab sessions.
